title: Visualizing orbital magnetism in electron doped rhombohedral multilayer graphene
authors: Owen I. Sheekey, Trevor B. Arp, Benjamin A. Foutty, Ruoxi Zhang, Tixuan Tan, Ludwig F. W. Holleis, Yi Guo, Sandesh S. Kalantre, Canxun Zhang, Mark Zakharyan, David Gong, Aidan Keough, Youngjoon Choi, Ysun Choi, Siyuan Xu, Tian Xie, Ben Hodder Alexander, Marisa Hocking, Qingrui Cao, Martin E. Huber, Takashi Taniguchi, Kenji Watanabe, Chenhao Jin, Etienne Lantagne-Hurtubise, Aaron Sharpe, Trithep Devakul, Andrea F. Young
abstract: Electron doped rhombohedral multilayer graphene at high displacement field features an exceptionally flat band minimum with near-ideal quantum geometry. Experiments in this regime observe the formation of a 'quarter metal,' in which the electron liquid condenses into a single spin- and valley flavor. Remarkably, recent experiments have found a zero resistance state in the same region of the density- and displacement-field-tuned parameter space, attributed to the formation of a chiral superconductor characterized by a finite-momentum Cooper pair condensate. Here, we use nanoSQUID-on-tip magnetometry to map the orbital magnetization of electron-doped rhombohedral graphene devices ranging in thickness between 3 and 13 layers. Magnetization within the quarter metal phases peaks at finite density, consistent with concentration of the Berry curvature in a finite-momentum 'ring of fire'. Correlating transport and local magnetometry data in a tetralayer sample reveals that the superconducting state has a finite orbital magnetic moment, providing direct evidence of its chiral nature. We further show that widely observed stochastic switching of the resistivity in the metallic regime arises from a density-tuned sign change in the valley-resolved total magnetic moment. This leads to the formation of metastable magnetic domains under typical gate control sequences and can also be harnessed for electric-field controlled switching of orbital moment across the entire device. Unexpectedly, we find magnetic inhomogeneity specific to the apparent normal state of the chiral superconductor, suggestive of a strain-tuned competition between magnetic and non-magnetic ground states. Our results point to a subtle energetic competition underlying the observation of chiral superconductivity in a narrow range of layer numbers.
summary: This work investigates orbital magnetism and chiral superconductivity in multilayer graphene using nanoSQUID magnetometry. By correlating local magnetization measurements with transport data, the authors confirm the chiral nature of the superconducting state and reveal that the system's magnetic behavior is governed by the concentration of Berry curvature. The findings highlight a complex interplay between quantum geometry, doping, and superconducting pairing in these advanced 2D materials.
main_problem: The study aims to map and understand the orbital magnetization in electron-doped rhombohedral multilayer graphene, particularly in relation to its quantum geometric properties and superconducting states.
main_result: The research provides direct evidence of chiral superconductivity via a finite orbital magnetic moment and demonstrates that the orbital magnetization is highly sensitive to carrier density and displacement field due to Berry curvature concentration.
method: The work combines advanced local magnetometry (nanoSQUID-on-tip) measurements with theoretical calculations based on continuum Hamiltonians to model the system's electronic structure.
model_or_system: The physical system is electron-doped rhombohedral multilayer graphene (RMG) with thicknesses ranging from 3 to 13 layers. The theoretical modeling uses a continuum Hamiltonian incorporating interlayer hopping and electrostatic screening.
key_observables: Orbital magnetization ($\Delta B$), zero-resistance state (superconductivity), Berry curvature concentration ('Berry ring of fire'), and magnetic domain patterns.
important_parameters: Electron density ($n_e$), perpendicular displacement field ($D$), and layer number ($N=3$ to $13$).
main_assumptions: The interpretation of transport anomalies relies on assumptions about sample homogeneity, and the study suggests a subtle energetic competition underlies the observed chiral superconductivity.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate $\Delta B$ measurements showing magnetization peaks at finite density, comparisons between orbital ferromagnetism and chiral superconductivity, and spatial mapping of magnetic inhomogeneity in the superconducting regime.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing the orbital magnetization using magnetometry across varying doping/field regimes, to linking this magnetism to the superconducting state, and finally investigating the complex phase competition and domain formation in the chiral superconducting region.
why_interesting: The strong coupling between orbital magnetism (a topological/geometric property) and superconductivity in a 2D material like graphene is highly relevant to understanding exotic superconducting states and topological superconductivity, which are key areas in condensed matter theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Each line represents a single gate sweep from low to high ne. Curves are colored according to their resistance at ne = 2.25 × 1012cm−2: curves with R < 100 ohms are colored gold, curves with R > 100 ohms are colored blue. (b) δB measured simultaneously with the transport in panel a at the point indicated in the inset to panel a. Color coding is consistent with panel a; i.e., by the resistance value.
